body_txt: The Hollywood version of "Waste Not, Want Not". When noticeable Costumes or Props are made for one production, they may turn up again and again in other productions that otherwise wouldn't have the budget to design and build their own. The prop may be altered slightly by repainting it , adding or removing widgets, combining different parts with another prop or used as background filler but it usually remains recognizable, especially if the original production and prop was well known. While an understandable cost-saving measure, low effort "stock props" makes the practice just scream " low budget ". Compare Recycled Set , which is entire sets and backlots that are shared and will usually retain some recognizable features. Can overlap with Whole-Costume Reference if the costume literally comes from the earlier work. Off-the-Shelf FX is when the recycled item is a commercially available device that was reimagined as something else. Traced Artwork is when it borrows layout and design directly from a previous piece. There is also Classic Filler Text , in which a newspaper is made for a specific production but most of the article text is filler text that has been used in other productions. For when video games reuse "models" of the polygonal kind in the same work, see You ALL Look Familiar and New Work, Recycled Graphics . Compare Stock Footage , Recycled Animation , and California Doubling . If an existing prop is being used to stand in for a different type of prop, it's an Asset Actor . This is utterly ubiquitous in live theatre, so a section for this would be hopelessly long, and utterly pointless. Just take it as a given that live theatre companies save and re-use everything.
